text.title: Contextual Inquiry: Six-Step Process
text.summary: The six operational steps: prepare, introduce, observe-and-interview, capture data, debrief, and cross-session analysis.

rule.statement: Define focus areas (which aspects of work to observe), recruit 4-8 participants for one focus area, obtain organizational permission, prepare recording equipment, and create an observation guide before the first session.
rule.rationale: 4-8 sessions is the sweet spot for one focus area — enough to find patterns across participants without spending more sessions than new insights warrant.

rule.statement: Run a 1-hour pilot before the real round to fix observation-guide weaknesses. The second guide is always better.
rule.rationale: Guides written before observation contain leading questions and missing focus areas that only become visible when used in the field.

rule.statement: Watch silently first, then ask about specific observed actions: "Why did you do that?" Never suggest improvements or express surprise at workarounds during the session.
rule.rationale: Premature suggestions and expressed surprise alter behavior immediately. The session value depends on observing what the user actually does, not what they think they should do with a researcher watching.

rule.statement: Capture artifacts physically (photos with consent). Workflows rarely live entirely on screens; paper notes, whiteboard scribbles, and handwritten forms are primary data.
rule.rationale: Remote CI variants cannot capture physical artifacts, which is a significant data loss. In-person sessions must compensate by actively documenting the physical environment.

rule.statement: Debrief within 30 minutes of each session, while peripheral memory is intact. Audio recording does not replace immediate debrief — peripheral observations and interpretive reactions are not on the recording.
rule.rationale: Peripheral memory (environmental details, emotional tone, gestural context) decays within hours. Recordings capture only what the microphone picked up.

rule.statement: After all sessions: identify patterns using affinity diagrams (cluster observations across participants by theme), sequence models (map workflow steps, decisions, artifacts, handoffs), and cultural models (power dynamics, informal processes, values).
rule.rationale: Single-session insights are anecdotes. Cross-session analysis transforms observations into findings. Skipping analysis leaves data in notebooks with no downstream value.

rule.statement: Share interim findings with participants ("member checking") to catch misinterpretations before downstream design work commits to them.
rule.rationale: Researchers form incorrect interpretations even with shared-interpretation protocol. Participant validation is the final check before findings become design inputs.

text.reference: Observation row dataclass for cross-session analysis
